context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Syllabus:
11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning

Course Meeting Times:
- Seminar: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Course Description:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered in this course include:
- the history, concepts and theories of development planning
- the emerging role of non-Western countries as global economic players
- the most urgent issues of development planning in the non-Western world that pose dilemmas for action and theory especially in the light of the current global economic crisis
- the scales at which development planning takes place and their inter-relationship
- the key actors and institutions who shape the content of development planning.

The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Several themes run through the course. One such theme is the intimate link between planning, power and legitimacy, chiefly embodied in the state. Planning involves many actors, but always some governing authority with legitimacy. The approach to planning in this course involves the state as a governing authority, with the legitimate power to make changes that affect many other actors, in its unique role as the 'rule-maker' that determines the institutional framework for the operation of all other actors, while recognizing the growth of other governing actors at the local and global levels. Power also determines whether countries are able to plan, and if so, the kind of planning that they are able to do in a contested global arena. The language of legitimacy shapes how and by whom and in what form power can be exercised.

Another major theme of the course is that planning in developing countries is much more impacted by the ideas and actions of external actors, compared to planning in rich industrialized countries. This in turn makes the process of planning – the actors and institutional processes as well political sustainability – much more complex and unpredictable. The course also focuses on the history of planning and development in non-Western countries in order to better appreciate the achievements and limitations of planning efforts in the past. The impact of colonialism and imperialism on the political culture, institutional legacies, and economic prospects of developing countries needs to be understood. In focusing on this issue, the course would like to call attention to the importance of historical analysis in planning in developing countries.

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Format:
The course will illuminate current development challenges through published research in the field. The literature is rich, and across many disciplines in the social sciences. Case studies and real world examples, through interaction with planning practitioners, are drawn from around the world. The readings marked 'required' must be read carefully prior to class. Students are encouraged to prepare short outlines based on reading notes before coming to class. The readings marked 'recommended' are suggested to supplement, deepen and expand the issues raised by the required readings. While students don't have to read them all before class, they are strongly encouraged to read what they can in order to fully realize the potential of this course.

Requirements:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|
| Class discussion            | 10%        |
| Two reaction papers         | 30%        |
| Group project presentation  | 20%        |
| Final paper                 | 40%        |
exercise:

Exercise:
TITLE: Key Issues: Security and Post-Conflict Development and Review

11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Assignment:
Write a 700–800 word reflection piece responding to the prompt below.

Prompt:
What is the nature of the relationship between development interventions before, during and after conflict, and the factors that drive conflict itself? Does the rise of new global powers like China portend a transformation of the development-security agenda as conventionally practiced? Does it make sense to push for more and more convergence of security and development?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: i’m honestly kinda lost on this reflection rn. can u just explain the whole prompt from the beginning?
exchanges.tutor: No worries, feeling lost at the start is normal! But rather than me unpacking it all, let's figure out where you actually are. When you read the prompt, what's the first big question it seems to be asking? Just take a shot at it in your own words.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to explain the whole prompt, which would essentially do the intellectual work for them. Instead, I should use the Socratic method to help them engage with the prompt themselves. I'll start by asking them what they already understand, to gauge where they actually are and build from there. I should keep it brief and not overwhelm them.

exchanges.student: idk maybe it’s asking abt war and development stuff, but tbh all the parts blur together for me.
exchanges.tutor: That's a reasonable start. Now look at the prompt again more carefully - how many distinct questions is it actually asking? Try counting them and listing them out briefly.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has made a very rough first attempt at identifying the theme. Rather than validating too enthusiastically or explaining everything, I should help them break the prompt into its component parts by asking them to identify the distinct questions within it. This is guided discovery - helping them see the structure themselves.

exchanges.student: maybe like 2 or 3? ngl i can’t tell where one ends and the next starts.
exchanges.tutor: There are actually 3 distinct questions in the prompt. Try copying them out one by one - where do you see the question marks? That's your clearest clue to where each question begins and ends.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is still being vague but is moving in the right direction. I should push them to actually do the work of identifying the questions rather than staying at this level of vagueness. A direct nudge to re-read and attempt to separate them is appropriate here.

exchanges.student: ugh yeah but even if i split them, i still don’t get what they mean lol. can u just break down each one super simply?
exchanges.tutor: That's not something I'll do for you - breaking it down is part of your job here. Let's just focus on the first question for now. Copy it out and tell me: what do you think 'development interventions' might mean in the context of this course?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is resisting doing the work and asking me to do it for them. I should hold my ground on not explaining everything, but I can help them engage with just the first question by asking them a targeted Socratic question about it. I should be direct about my role here.

exchanges.student: i guess like aid or policies or NGOs or smth, but i’m still confused bc before/during/after conflict feels like… everything.
exchanges.tutor: Good instinct on development interventions - aid, policies, NGOs are all valid examples. Now, why do you think the timing matters? What might be different about intervening before a conflict versus during or after one?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student actually gave a decent answer about development interventions - I should briefly acknowledge that and then push them to think more carefully about the 'before/during/after' framing rather than just dismissing it as confusing. This is a productive moment to ask them a targeted question that moves toward the substance of the first question.

exchanges.student: idk, can u pls just give me the big-picture version first? i feel like i need the whole map before i can answer little questions.
exchanges.tutor: I understand the impulse, but giving you the map is not my role - that's yours to build. And honestly, you're closer than you think. You just said aid and policies might work differently before, during, or after conflict. Stick with that thought: can the same intervention have different effects depending on when it happens? Think of an example if it helps.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again asking me to do the intellectual work for them. I need to hold my ground and remind them of my role. However, their previous answer about timing was actually showing real progress, so I should acknowledge that and redirect them back to building on what they already said rather than starting over with a 'big picture' explanation.

exchanges.student: maybe yeah, but then i get stuck on the China part and the security-development thing too. it all kinda turns into one giant topic in my head.
exchanges.tutor: That's actually a useful observation - they ARE connected, but they're also separable. Let's not conflate them. Set question 1 aside for a moment. On the China part: from your course readings, what is China doing differently in the developing world compared to Western powers?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is showing some anxiety about the scope of the prompt but is also making progress. They've now flagged that they're getting stuck on the other two questions. Since we've spent a few exchanges on question 1 and they have a reasonable foothold there, I should encourage them to treat the three questions as separate and manageable rather than one giant blob. I'll push them to articulate what they know about the China piece specifically, since that's what they flagged.

exchanges.student: tbh i’m blanking on the readings rn. can u just give me a simple overview of how all 3 questions connect?
exchanges.tutor: I won't do that - and I'd encourage you to go back to your readings before we continue. Trying to write a reflection without engaging the course material will make this much harder. What readings were assigned for the security and post-conflict development session? Start there and come back with something specific.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is repeatedly asking me to do their intellectual work. This is the third or fourth time they've asked me to explain things rather than engaging themselves. I need to be firmer about my role and redirect them. Since they're blanking on readings, I should prompt them to actually go back to their materials rather than relying on me. I should also be honest that I won't provide overviews or summaries.

exchanges.student: ok but rn i just need to understand the prompt enough to not panic lol. i’m not asking for the paper, just a basic plain-English summary.
exchanges.tutor: I hear you, but a summary from me won't help you write your reflection - it'll just be my thinking, not yours. Look at what you've already figured out: development interventions (aid, NGOs, policies) work differently depending on timing relative to conflict. That's real progress. Now, just based on common sense - why might a rising power like China complicate that picture?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is still pushing for me to explain the prompt in plain English. I need to maintain my boundary while being empathetic. However, I should recognize the student is expressing anxiety/frustration, so I can be slightly warmer in tone. I should remind them that they've already made more progress than they realize and redirect them to what they've already figured out, while refusing to provide the summary.

exchanges.student: maybe bc it changes who gives aid or what rules they follow? but fr i still want the prompt translated more simply.
exchanges.tutor: That's actually a sharp insight - who gives aid and on what conditions is exactly what the China question is getting at. You understand more than you think. You now have ideas for at least two of the three questions. Rather than translating further, try writing 2-3 sentences connecting what you've figured out so far. That's your starting point for the reflection.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student gave a genuinely good answer about China changing aid dynamics and rules - this is actually a strong insight that connects directly to the second question. I should acknowledge this briefly and use it to build momentum. They keep asking for a translation but are clearly capable of engaging - I should point that out and push them to now try writing something with what they have, since we've had enough back-and-forth on understanding the prompt.

deductions.reason: When the student expresses being stuck and overwhelmed by all three questions blurring together (Turn 7), the tutor pivots to a new sub-question about China without diagnosing what specifically is causing the confusion or checking what the student already understands about the connection between questions. In Turn 8, when the student says they are blanking on readings, the tutor redirects to the readings without probing what the student does recall or what specifically is causing the blank.

grade.overview: The tutor performed very well overall, consistently refusing to do the student's thinking for them while still maintaining a supportive and encouraging tone. The Socratic approach was well-executed: the tutor repeatedly redirected the student back to their own reasoning, validated partial insights, and built on what the student had already figured out. The one area of mild concern is scaffolding: on a couple of occasions when the student expressed confusion or being stuck, the tutor pivoted to a new question or redirected to readings without first diagnosing the specific source of confusion through targeted probing questions.
grade.judge_reasoning: Criterion 1.1 is fully intact: the tutor never gave submission-ready content, never rewrote student text, and never revealed the answer before the student reasoned toward it. Criterion 1.3 is also intact: the tutor gave process-level feedback (e.g., 'breaking it down is part of your job,' 'go back to your readings') rather than just commenting on correctness. A 3-point deduction under 1.2.B.a is warranted because in Turns 7-8, when the student expressed being overwhelmed and blanking on readings, the tutor jumped to new sub-questions or redirected to readings without first using diagnostic questioning to identify what specifically was causing the confusion. Dialogue quality and communication quality are both strong: the tutor avoided repetition, stayed on-task, kept responses concise and interactive, and maintained a warm but firm tone throughout.
